subject: Physics
q: If the potential difference between B and D is zero, the value of x is 1n Ω. The value of n is ______.
question: If the potential difference between B and D is zero, the value of x is 1n Ω. The value of n is ______.
solution:
As potential difference between B & D is zero therefore it is a balanced wheatstone bridge. So, we can write

⇒R1R2=R3R4⇒6×36+31+2=x×1x+1x

⇒23=1x+1

⇒x=0.5=12

Hence, required n=2.

subject: Physics
q: Equivalent resistance of the following network is ________ Ω.
question: Equivalent resistance of the following network is ________ Ω.
solution:
In the above diagram, 6Ω is short circuit. Therefore, we can remove it.

In the left part, 2 Ω and 2 Ω are in parallel combination, which will give us 1 Ω which again in turn is in series combination with the 2 Ω(adjacent to 3 Ω resistor), therefore net contribution of this part will be 1 Ω+2 Ω=3 Ω. 

Now, all three 3 Ω are in parallel across point A & B, therefore Req=3×13=1 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Resistance of a wire at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $t^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is found to be $10 \Omega, 10.2 \Omega$ and $10.95 \Omega$ respectively. The temperature $t$ in Kelvin scale is _________
question: Resistance of a wire at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $t^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is found to be $10 \Omega, 10.2 \Omega$ and $10.95 \Omega$ respectively. The temperature $t$ in Kelvin scale is _________
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_0(1+\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}) \\ & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}_0}=\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}\end{aligned}$
Case-I
$\begin{aligned} & 0{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow 100^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & \frac{10.2-10}{10}=\alpha(100-0)\end{aligned}$   ...(1)
Case-II
$0^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{t}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\frac{10.95-10}{10}=\alpha(\mathrm{t}-0)$   ...(2)
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{t}}{100}=\frac{0.95}{0.2}=475^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & \mathrm{t}=475+273=748 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A heating element has a resistance of <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> at room temperature. When it is connected to a supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, a steady current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes in it and temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><msup><mo> </mo><mi>o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> more than the room temperature. The temperature coefficient of resistance of the heating element is
question: A heating element has a resistance of <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> at room temperature. When it is connected to a supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, a steady current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes in it and temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><msup><mo> </mo><mi>o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> more than the room temperature. The temperature coefficient of resistance of the heating element is
solution:
Resistance of heating element after temperature increases by 500 oC=2202=110 Ω (from Ohm's law). The new resistance is related to the temperature coefficient of the heating element as 110=100 (1+α500)

α=10100 ×500⇒α=2 ×10-4 °C-1

subject: Physics
q: The combination of two identical cells, whether connected in series or parallel combination provides the same current through an external resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The value of internal resistance of each cell is
question: The combination of two identical cells, whether connected in series or parallel combination provides the same current through an external resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The value of internal resistance of each cell is
solution:
When the cells are connected in series is=2ε2r+R=2ε2r+2

When they are connected in parallel, the emf is εp=εr+εr1r+1r=ε and the effective internal resistance will be rp=r×rr+r=r2. Therefore, the current will be,

ip=εR+r2=ε2+r2=2ε4+r

Since, is=ip⇒2ε2r+2=2ε4+r⇒r=2 Ω

subject: Physics
q: What will be the most suitable combination of three resistors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> so that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is equivalent resistance of combination?
question: What will be the most suitable combination of three resistors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> so that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is equivalent resistance of combination?
options: Parallel combination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> connected in series with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>

Parallel combination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> connected in series with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>

Series combination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> connected in parallel with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>.

Series combination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> connected in parallel with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>.
solution:
If the 2 Ω resistance and the 4 Ω resistance are connected in parallel to each other, then R'=4×24+2=43 Ω. If the resistance of 6 Ω is now connected in series to the previous combination.

Req=2×42+4+6=223 Ω

question: If an ammeter is to be used in place of a voltmeter, then we must connect with the ammeter a

q: Two cells are connected between points A and B as shown. Cell 1 has emf of 12 V and internal resistance of 3 Ω. Cell 2 has emf of 6 V and internal resistance of 6 Ω. An external resistor R of 4 Ω is connected across A and B. The current flowing through R will be ______ A.
question: Two cells are connected between points A and B as shown. Cell 1 has emf of 12 V and internal resistance of 3 Ω. Cell 2 has emf of 6 V and internal resistance of 6 Ω. An external resistor R of 4 Ω is connected across A and B. The current flowing through R will be ______ A.
solution:
Equivalent internal resistance of two cells in parallel is req=r1r2r1+r2=3×63+6=2 Ω

Equivalent emf of given cells 

 Eeq=E1r1-E2r2req=123-662=6 V

Given: R=4 Ω

The circuit can be redrawn as shown below.

Current through resistor R is I=62+4=1 A.

subject: Physics
q: In the circuit, given in the figure currents in different branches and value of one resistor are shown. Then potential at point B with respect to the point A is:
question: In the circuit, given in the figure currents in different branches and value of one resistor are shown. Then potential at point B with respect to the point A is:
solution:
Using kirchhoff's law in the circuit ACDB, we get

Voltage at B= voltage at A + voltage of battery of 1 V + potential difference across 2 Ω resistance - voltage of battery of 2 V

⇒VA+1+2(1)-2=VB

Now potential at point B with respect to point A is given by,

⇒VB-VA=1 V

subject: Physics
q: A galvanometer having a coil resistance of <math><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> gives a full scale deflection, when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> is passed through it. The value of the resistance, which can convert this galvanometer into ammeter giving a full scale deflection for a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, is:
question: A galvanometer having a coil resistance of <math><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> gives a full scale deflection, when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> is passed through it. The value of the resistance, which can convert this galvanometer into ammeter giving a full scale deflection for a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, is:
solution:
As shown in figure a shunt resistance S is connected parallel to the galvanometer to convert it into an ammeter. Let G is the resistance of the galvanometer.

Where G = 100 Ω and full-scale deflection current is Ig = 10 mA = 10×10-3 A

For galvanometer Vg = Ig G = 10×10-3 ×10 = 0.1 volt

From the figure, the shunt is parallel to galvanometer

∴VS = Vg ⇒IS S = Vg   ...1

For ammeter full-scale deflection current is 

IS =I-Ig ≈ 10 A

∴ from equation 1

10 S = 0·1 ⇒S = 0·01 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A dc source of emf $\mathrm{E}_1=100 \mathrm{~V}$ and internal resistance $r=0.5 \Omega$, a storage battery of emf $\mathrm{E}_2=90 \mathrm{~V}$ and an external resistance $\mathrm{R}$ are connected as shown in figure. For what value of R no current will pass through the battery?
question: A dc source of emf $\mathrm{E}_1=100 \mathrm{~V}$ and internal resistance $r=0.5 \Omega$, a storage battery of emf $\mathrm{E}_2=90 \mathrm{~V}$ and an external resistance $\mathrm{R}$ are connected as shown in figure. For what value of R no current will pass through the battery?
solution:
$\frac{100}{R+r}=\frac{90}{R}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{10}{9} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 1+\frac{0.5}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{10}{9} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{0.5}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{9} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{R}=4.5 \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

q: A metal wire of resistance <math><mn>3</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is elongated to make a uniform wire of double its previous length. This new wire is now bent and the ends joined to make a circle. If two points on this circle make an angle <math><msup><mrow><mn>60</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> at the center, the equivalent resistance between these two points will be:
question: A metal wire of resistance <math><mn>3</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is elongated to make a uniform wire of double its previous length. This new wire is now bent and the ends joined to make a circle. If two points on this circle make an angle <math><msup><mrow><mn>60</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> at the center, the equivalent resistance between these two points will be:
solution:
We know, R=ρlA=ρl2Al=ρl2Volume
R∝l2 , if the volume is constant.
∴  New resistance =4R

R1=4R360×300=10R3
R2=4R360×60=2R3
∴ Equivalent resistance Req=R1R2R1+R2
=10R3×2R310R3+2R3=5R9
=53 Ω

q: A uniform metallic wire carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery is connected across it. The mass of uniform metallic wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and resistivity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>  The length of wire is :
question: A uniform metallic wire carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery is connected across it. The mass of uniform metallic wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and resistivity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>  The length of wire is :
solution:
Given here, I=2 A, V=3.4 V, m=8.92×10-3 kg,

d=8.92×103 kg m-3 and ρ=1.7×10-8 Ω-m

From Ohm's law, resistance is R=VI=3.42=1.7 Ω

As resistance of wire is given by R=ρlA, where, A is area and l is length of wire.

 ⇒1.7=ρlA ⇒l=1.7Aρ

Now, volume=md=8.92×10-38.92×103=10-6 m3

Thus, 

l2=1.7Aρ×l=1.7volumelρ×l=1.7ρ10-6=1.71.7×10-8×10-6

Hence, l=10 m

subject: Physics
q: In a metre-bridge when a resistance in the left gap is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and unknown resistance in the right gap, the balance length is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> On shunting the unknown resistance with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the balance length changes by:
question: In a metre-bridge when a resistance in the left gap is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and unknown resistance in the right gap, the balance length is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> On shunting the unknown resistance with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the balance length changes by:
solution:
In first circuit:

For balanced condition, we get 240=X60⇒X=3Ω

In second case:

Net resistance of the right side due to shunting becomes,  X'=2×32+3=1.2 Ω

Again for balanced condition, 

2ℓ=1.2100-ℓ

⇒200-2ℓ=1.2ℓ

⇒ℓ=2003.2=62.5 cm

Clearly, balance length changes by 22.5 cm.

subject: Physics
q: Two voltameters one of copper and another of silver, are joined in parallel. When a total charge $q$ flows through the voltameters, equal amount of metals are deposited. If the electrochemical equivalents of copper and silver are $z_1$ and $z_2$ respectively the charge which flows through the silver voltameter is
question: Two voltameters one of copper and another of silver, are joined in parallel. When a total charge $q$ flows through the voltameters, equal amount of metals are deposited. If the electrochemical equivalents of copper and silver are $z_1$ and $z_2$ respectively the charge which flows through the silver voltameter is
solution:
$q_1 Z_1=q_2 Z_2$
$q=q_1+q_2$
$\therefore q_2=\frac{q}{1+\frac{Z_2}{Z_1}}$

q: In the given figure an ammeter A consists of a $240 \Omega$ coil connected in parallel to a $10 \Omega$ shunt. The reading of the ammeter is $\qquad$ $\mathrm{mA}$
question: In the given figure an ammeter A consists of a $240 \Omega$ coil connected in parallel to a $10 \Omega$ shunt. The reading of the ammeter is $\qquad$ $\mathrm{mA}$
solution: Req $\begin{aligned} & =140.4+\frac{240 \times 10}{240+10} \\ & \text { Req }=140.4+\frac{2400}{250} \\ & \text { Req. }=150 \Omega \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Current in ammeter } & =\frac{24}{150} \\ & =160 \mathrm{~mA}\end{aligned}$

q: A resistor develops <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of thermal energy in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through it. If the current increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the energy developed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: A resistor develops <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> of thermal energy in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through it. If the current increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the energy developed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
Energy produced is given by H=i2Rt

When i=2 A, t=15 s, energy is H=300=22×R×15

Resistance is R=5 Ω

When i=3 A, t=10 s.

Energy is H'=32×5×10=450 J

We can draw the resultant circuit as follows:

subject: Physics
q: To compare EMF of two cells using potentiometer the balancing lengths obtained are 200 cm and 150 cm. The least count of scale is 1 cm. The percentage error in the ratio of EMFs is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: To compare EMF of two cells using potentiometer the balancing lengths obtained are 200 cm and 150 cm. The least count of scale is 1 cm. The percentage error in the ratio of EMFs is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
$\epsilon = \lambda \ell$ (Potential gradient)

$\epsilon_1 = \lambda \ell_1$
$\epsilon_2 = \lambda \ell_2$

$y = \frac{\epsilon_1}{\epsilon_2} = \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}$

$\frac{\Delta y}{y} = \frac{\Delta \ell_1}{\ell_1} + \frac{\Delta \ell_2}{\ell_2}$

$\frac{\Delta y}{y} = \frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{150}$

$\frac{\Delta y}{y} \times 100 = \left(\frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{150}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{3+4}{600}\right) \times 100 = \frac{7}{6} = 1.16\%$

subject: Physics
q: Two identical heater filaments are connected first in parallel and then in series. At the same applied voltage, the ratio of heat produced in same time for parallel to series will be:
question: Two identical heater filaments are connected first in parallel and then in series. At the same applied voltage, the ratio of heat produced in same time for parallel to series will be:
solution:
Let the resistance of the filaments be R and the voltage be V.

For the series combination the power is 

P1=V22R 

For parallel combination,

P2=V2R2=2V2R

Hence, the ratio is

⇒P2P1=41

subject: Physics
q: Two cells of the same EMF E but different internal resistances r1 and r2 are connected in series with an external resistance R as shown in the figure. The terminal potential difference across, the second cell is found to be zero. The external resistance R must then be:
question: Two cells of the same EMF E but different internal resistances r1 and r2 are connected in series with an external resistance R as shown in the figure. The terminal potential difference across, the second cell is found to be zero. The external resistance R must then be:
solution:
In this question we have to find the Terminal potential difference of second cell, we are given the internal resistances of both the cells and an external resistance. Applying the following expression:-

V = E-ir2

⇒0=E-ir2

Applying the expression of current

0=E-2Er1+r2+R·r2

⇒R=r2-r1

q:
In the circuit diagrams (A, B, C and D) shown below, $\mathrm{R}$ is a high resistance and $\mathrm{S}$ is a resistance of the order of galvanometer resistance G. question:
In the circuit diagrams (A, B, C and D) shown below, $\mathrm{R}$ is a high resistance and $\mathrm{S}$ is a resistance of the order of galvanometer resistance G. The correct circuit, corresponding to the half deflection method for finding the resistance and figure of merit of the galvanometer, is the circuit labelled as: (a)
(b)
(C)
(d)
solution:
The correct circuit diagram is $D$ with galvanometer resistance
$$
G=\frac{R S}{R-S}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A constant voltage is applied between two ends of a metallic wire. If the length is halved and the radius of the wire is doubled, the rate of heat developed in the wire will be:
question: A constant voltage is applied between two ends of a metallic wire. If the length is halved and the radius of the wire is doubled, the rate of heat developed in the wire will be:
solution:
Rate of heat i.e., Power developed in the
$$
\text { wire }=P=\frac{V^2}{R}
$$
Resistance of the wire of length, $L$
$$
R_1=\frac{\rho L}{A}=\frac{\rho L}{\pi r^2}
$$
$\therefore$ Power, $P_1=\frac{V^2}{R_1}$
Resistance of the wire when length is halved i.e., $L / 2$
$$
R_2=\frac{\rho \frac{L}{2}}{\pi(2 r)^2}=\frac{\rho L}{\pi 8 r^2}=\frac{R_1}{8}
$$
$\therefore$ Power, $P_2=\frac{V}{\frac{R_1}{8}}=\frac{8 V}{R_1}$
or, $\mathrm{P}_2=8 \mathrm{P}_1$ i.e., power increased 8 times of previous or original wire.

subject: Physics
q: A <math><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance is connected to a battery of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>V</mi></math>. A galvanometer of resistance <math><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is to be used as an ammeter to measure current through the resistance, for this a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></msub></math> is connected to the galvanometer. Which of the following connections should be employed if the measured current is with in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the current without the ammeter in the circuit?
question: A <math><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance is connected to a battery of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>V</mi></math>. A galvanometer of resistance <math><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is to be used as an ammeter to measure current through the resistance, for this a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></msub></math> is connected to the galvanometer. Which of the following connections should be employed if the measured current is with in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the current without the ammeter in the circuit?
options: <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>  in series with galvanometer

<math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>  in parallel with galvanometer

<math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> in parallel with the galvanometer
solution:
Initially current in the circuit is 

I=550=0.1

and the current in circuit when ammeter is connected,is 1 % of initial current , i.e.,  I′=1100× 0·1 = 0.099 A

To increase the range of galvanometer, let resistance rs
 is connected parallel to it and along with 50 Ω is connected in series to ammeter. So, equivalent resistance  of circuit is:

Req=50+100 rS100+rS

Now, From equation V= I' Req

5 = 0.099×50+100 rs100+rs⇒50+100 rs100+rs = 50.099⇒50+100 rs100+rs =50.5050⇒100 rs100+rs = 50.5050⇒rs = 0.5 Ω

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of heat dissipated per second through the resistance $5 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$ in the circuit given below is :
question: The ratio of heat dissipated per second through the resistance $5 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$ in the circuit given below is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{i}_1}{\mathrm{i}_2}=\frac{10}{5}=\frac{2}{1} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{P}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{i}_1^2 \mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{i}_2^2 \mathrm{R}_2}=\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)^2 \times \frac{5}{10}=\frac{2}{1}\end{aligned}$

solution:
As can be seen from the first circuit above, CB & AB are in series which is in parallel with CA. So, the net resistance is

R'=62 Ω=3  Ω as shown in the second diagram.

From the second diagram, DC & CA is in series which is parallel to DA. Hence the resistance is

R''=6×3(6+3) Ω=2 Ω as shown in third diagram.

From third diagram, both ED & DA are in series which is parallel to EA.

The equivalent resistance as can be seen from the above simplified circuit is Req=2 Ω.

 The current I through battery, I= 82=4 A.

From first diagram, when the resistances are in parallel the current is divided in the inverse ratio. Thus,
I' through CD=39×2=23 A

subject: Physics
q: A student is provided with a variable voltage source V, a test resistor RT=10Ω, two identical galvanometers G1 and G2 and two additional resistors, R1=10MΩ and R2=0.001Ω. For conducting an experiment to verify ohm's law, the most suitable circuit is:
question: A student is provided with a variable voltage source V, a test resistor RT=10Ω, two identical galvanometers G1 and G2 and two additional resistors, R1=10MΩ and R2=0.001Ω. For conducting an experiment to verify ohm's law, the most suitable circuit is:
solution:
A galvanometer is a tool used to either detect or quantify an electric current. The movement of a magnetic needle or a coil in a magnetic field, which is an essential component of a galvanometer, typically indicates the current and its strength.

Depending up on the resistance and the connectivity in the circuit, galvanometer can be used either as voltmeter or as an ammeter.

Voltmeter should have very high resistance, while ammeter should be having very less resistance.

Hence, the high resistor R1 should be connected in series with the galvanometer in order to measure the potential difference across the test resistor. Also, the low resistor R2 should be connected in parallel with the galvanometer in order to measure the current across the test resistor.

Thus, this combination will work properly.
